[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/nicotine-gum-biohack-ubi-17.15797/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/nicotine-gum-biohack-ubi-17.15797/)

> What about nicotine? Nicotine works best with good O2 consumption by mitochondria and strong natural full spectrum sunlight. Most nicotine users smoke so they never see the optimized effect of nicotine because of co-morbid lung disease from smoking. We can use a nicotrol inhaler to offset that risk. Most people who are sick rarely go out in the sun because their doctors or their beliefs keep them ignorant of basic physics. Going out in the sun makes us feel well because it causes release of beta endorphin from our skin. Runners get a high from this but the high is always greater in natural light. So how might nicotine work when O2 and UV light are optimized in your bio-hack? Nicotine slightly increases glucose levels to give a small superoxide pulse. This pulse make the perfect amount of singlet state ROS/superoxide to be the stimulus to lower the % of heteroplasmy in our cells. This small stimulus does two key things: 1. the small stimulus stimulates regeneration programs in us. 2. It stimulates mitophagy to recycle heteroplasmic poor functioning mitochondria to make new mitochondria and dig yourself out of the hole called mitochondria senescence. You cannot get the superoxide pulse without the O2 or the UV exposure. Did you know that DHA in your RBC's in skin arterioles 7-10 AM allows you to absorb even more UV light? This is possible because UVA light stimulates nitric oxide that vasodilators the skin arterioles to come closer to the surface so that UV light can be absorbed by porphyrins and hemoglobin in RBC's. UVB light penetrates only 0.1 mm. UVA penetrates 1-3 mm so to get UV light RBC's have to move toward the surface and they do because of the light nitric oxide couple. This is also why light makes plants move toward light too. If you're anemic, hypoxic, or diabetic, your fighting an uphill battle because your % of heteroplasmy is getting larger than it was when you were younger. Diseases can manifest just from the slow increase of heteroplasmy in mitochondria. But we can reverse the process if we get the natural details correct of how light works with chemicals in our body. Few people have that discipline in their bio-hacks. Some do. That is why we share what we have learned. All people as they age, they acquire this same kind of mito-nuclear coaptation dysfunction because as redox lowers, mitochondria uncouple and get more leaky and heteroplasmy increases. The major difference in aging compared to disease generation is that an aging person has a slower timescale of the development of heteroplasmy compared to those who develop illnesses at a younger age. The % of heteroplasmy is slower in natural aging, but faster in neolithic diseases in today's modern humans 20-40 years old. Their environment is driving the increased heteroplasmy. MB and nicotine can be used to combat this process.

### WalterNL — 2017-08-09T11:16:17+0300

# Thread: raising body temperature by exercising in the morning is effective for resetting circadian rhythms?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ValerieBee — 2017-08-08T21:13:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221205>

I thought I read or heard @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) recommend not to exercise right before or after sunrise? I can't remember where, and I've been wondering about this. I did see in [Blog CT7](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/) it appears mid-afternoon is prime time physically (funny enough, that is the time I know personally my brain seems to shut off, and my body wants to be active.)

Also does that statement make sense? ( [I happened across it](https://blog.bulletproof.com/does-grounding-work/) reading up on earthing.) Am I on the right path that the SCN is the mechanism of the body clock, meaning circadian rhythm? Is the tail wagging the dog on using temperature to change biorhythm, or what am I missing? I do see a bit of research that correlates body temp and circadian rhythm (and menstrual cycles), but of course correlation is not causation.

### kris90 — 2017-08-08T21:57:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221208>

I train first thing in the morning 2-3x per week with heavy weights. Because I am up so early before sunrise, I use cold as my stimulus, so after waking, and going outside to take a few deep breaths of fresh air, I hop in a cold shower, and it wakes me right up. Then I do some stretching and warm up, and onto the heavy weights. I end up getting my sunrise after my workout.

Haven't noticed any issues with this. I don't eat post workout either, usually push my first meal until around noon, and just drink lots of cold water. I like getting my work out done with in the morning, and I find it stimulates me for the day.

TBH, and this is just my opinion/experience, when you are leptin sensitive, I think you can program your circadian rhythms accordingly, as long as you keep them between sunrise and sunset. Ideally, I would like to exercise after sunrise, and I do on weekends, but with my damn job/schedule, I don't have this, so I have to biohack it, and find the cold works wonders for waking me up those early mornings.

### Penny — 2017-08-08T22:21:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221211>

> [kris90 said:](/goto/post?id=221208)
>
> I train first thing in the morning 2-3x per week with heavy weights. Because I am up so early before sunrise, I use cold as my stimulus, so after waking, and going outside to take a few deep breaths of fresh air, I hop in a cold shower, and it wakes me right up. Then I do some stretching and warm up, and onto the heavy weights. I end up getting my sunrise after my workout.
>
> Haven't noticed any issues with this. I don't eat post workout either, usually push my first meal until around noon, and just drink lots of cold water. I like getting my work out done with in the morning, and I find it stimulates me for the day.
>
> TBH, and this is just my opinion/experience, when you are leptin sensitive, I think you can program your circadian rhythms accordingly, as long as you keep them between sunrise and sunset. Ideally, I would like to exercise after sunrise, and I do on weekends, but with my damn job/schedule, I don't have this, so I have to biohack it, and find the cold works wonders for waking me up those early mornings.

I think it's because when cortisol spikes in the morning, it uses up electrons - so if you exercise at that time, maybe you're doing it with a half empty gas tank? But, in someone healthy, it's probably not a big deal - I used to swim at dawn outside and it was fine, but swimming indoors, I think it made me tired... I would go with what feels good - I also think this is why people have heart attacks in the morning - less electrons -

### kris90 — 2017-08-08T23:44:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221220>

Ya that makes a lot of sense. Ideally, on a day I don't have to work, I still enjoy my morning workouts, but I ease into them a little more by atleast engaging in them post-sunrise, and maybe a coffee beforehand.

That's why on days I cannot workout a little later, I'll use cold as my stimulus. Seems to work alright.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-08T23:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-221221>

### JohnnyAZ — 2017-08-22T08:31:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222027>

Before I found this site and I was blindly dealing with my issues I regularly worked out early in the AM. I did a cortisol spit test and when I did so I did one vial right after waking and my level was ok...not good...but ok. After I worked out I didn't feel well and did another vial early to see if my cortisol was affected. When the results came back the level on my post-workout had a drastically low cortisol level. It was below what it should have been at 9pm (based on the graph presented in the results). Since then I now workout later (based on Jack's advice in blog, forum, and webinars) and I do it from home where I can keep walking out into the sun and stand barefoot on the ground in between each set instead of in a gym under the blue lights. I don't have results on this as my most recent cortisol test was just sent in today but I do feel better throughout the day.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raising-body-temperature-by-exercising-in-the-morning-is-effective-for-resetting-circadian-rhythms.19985/post-222089>

I've noticed my basal body temperature has now dropped down to between 36.0 and 36.5. I feel lizard like, because my cold receptors are almost always activated (i.e. goosebumps & vasoconstriction) unless I bask in the sun, at which point vasodilation occurs very rapidly.

Yesterday, it was very hot and humid outside (35 C with the humidity). But because it was overcast and rainy, I still had goosebumps and vasoconstriction outside. My guess is that most of the UV light was blocked by cloud coverage.

The only other factor that reverses goosebumps and vasoconstriction is eating. And that makes sense giving that eating food is simply delivering light frequencies to the respiratory proteins. With the minimal clothing I wear, people think I'm warm all the time, but it's actually the opposite. I'm always cold until I have UV/IR light in my environment or through food. I've just learned to love feeling cold. Wim Hof breathing can be used to decrease feelings of cold by increasing oxygen uptake/tunneling more electrons through ETC and releasing more IR light in my mitochondria.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-08-10T16:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221348>

Infectious disease departments always wonder why they get occassional spikes in infections in ICU's. They also wonder why their nurse's use their healthcare more than expected. We see more resistant bacterial infections in the ICU than anyother place in healthcare. The reason is usually when new equipment enters the unit and is not properly grounded or emits massive electric or magnetic fields. If you bring in your testing gear when a spike in infection shows up it is pretty easy to find. No one realizes that excess nnEMF in the air around us acidifies water everywhere and that includes our cells. acidification lowers pH and helps infections to set up in cells because acidification of cells raises mitochondrial heteroplasmy rates. This is a great example of research done without TeleCom Industry funding showing us how nnEMF lower pH and can lead to infectious disease or to autoimmune conditions. [https://www.laboratoryequipment.com...=6045870&et\_rid=%%subscriberid%%&location=top](https://www.laboratoryequipment.com/news/2017/08/electrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies?et_cid=6045870&et_rid=45590457&location=top&linkid=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.laboratoryequipment.com%2Fnews%2F2017%2F08%2Felectrical-grounding-technique-may-improve-health-outcomes-nicu-babies%3Fet_cid%3D6045870%26et_rid%3D%25%25subscriberid%25%25%26location%3Dtop)

Aquaphotomics is the first step to understanding how the battery of life is built and how we can use it to find disease before it becomes deadly. [https://www.patreon.com/posts/13737092](https://www.patreon.com/posts/13737092)

### Scompy — 2017-08-11T04:17:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-rhythm-sepsis-the-mitos.19987/post-221398>

This was just a huge WOW to me...

"...glutamate-induced biophotonic emission does not necessarily correlate with the change in aerobic metabolism. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) can be produced by neural mitochondria as well as by the NADPH oxidase (4, 5). NADPH oxidase can produce ROS whether mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase is completely or incompletely blocked. Glutamate-induced ROS generation in neuronal presynaptic terminals is caused by the activation of the NADPH oxidase and nitric oxide synthases (6).

NMDA receptor (NMDAR) activation by the glutamate increases NADPH oxidase activity, which is the key source of superoxide formation (O2·−) (4). We conclude that the glutamate-induced NMDAR activation and NADPH oxidase activity can lead to overproduction of ROS that causes an increase of biophoton in the brain (7), which seems at odds with the reasoning behind the claims made by Wang et al. (2)."

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5035900/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5035900/)

No one realizes that excess nnEMF in the air around us acidifies water everywhere and that includes our cells. acidification lowers pH and helps infections to set up in cells because acidification of cells raises mitochondrial heteroplasmy rates. This is a great example of research done without TeleCom Industry funding showing us how nnEMF lower pH and can lead to infectious disease or to autoimmune conditions. My freind had an extended stay in the neurosurgery ward after the incision for his spinal operation was found not to be healing. He had to do a lot of reading (books) during his stay because they do not allow cellphones or laptops etc. on that ward. I think this speaks volumes.
